metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise AC2 to either (a) drop the `_commands/metrics.py` clause entirely (since that file is not delivered by this plan and ticket 9a5a is open) and add a note to ticket 9a5a to enforce the guard there, or (b) make the test conditional — skip/xfail when the file does not exist — and document that 9a5a must extend the guard; do not assert a vacuously-passing scan against a missing file.
evidence: Probed src/rebar/_commands/: no metrics.py file found — directory listing confirmed absence.
Ticket 9a5a status=open; src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py is not in the codebase.
AC2 text: 'the future _commands/metrics.py, once ticket 9a5a lands it, is held to the same rule'.
A test scanning a file that does not exist cannot assert anything meaningful about its imports; the assertion passes vacuously.
findings.finding: AC2's portability guard claims 'the future _commands/metrics.py, once ticket 9a5a lands it, is held to the same rule' — but src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py does not exist (ticket 9a5a is open/undelivered), so the test asserting its source does not import adapters.claude_transcripts trivially passes by scanning a missing file (zero content = zero forbidden imports). The guard the plan asserts for this file is unenforceable within this plan's delivery scope.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 (portability guard); 'the future _commands/metrics.py, once ticket 9a5a lands it, is held to the same rule'
scenarios: A developer runs the AC2 portability test: the test opens src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py, gets FileNotFoundError or skips, and the guard is never actually enforced — the core command later imports the adapter and no test catches it.
Ticket 9a5a lands after this ticket closes; the portability guard test added here is never updated to scan the now-real file, leaving a permanent coverage gap.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the `_commands/metrics.py` clause from AC2 in this ticket. Add an explicit note: 'ticket 9a5a must add a portability-guard test asserting src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py does not import rebar.metrics.adapters.' This keeps the guard accurate to what actually exists at the time the test runs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the Claude Code session transcript filesystem path (or a documented discovery mechanism such as `~/.claude/projects/*/` glob) and specify the JSONL record schema — at minimum which field(s) hold error-bearing text and which field holds the ISO-8601 timestamp — so the adapter and backfill script can be implemented without independent filesystem archaeology.
evidence: Plan: 'targeted pass: an error-signature grep over the transcript JSONL' — no path or discovery mechanism given
Plan emitted schema: `"ts": <ISO-8601 of the transcript entry>` — no JSONL field name given
docs/session-id-shims.md documents Codex transcript path (~/.codex/sessions/...) but no Claude Code transcript path appears anywhere in the repo
scripts/session-shims/claude-code-session-start.sh uses CLAUDE_ENV_FILE for env injection but contains no mention of where session transcripts are stored
Evidence class: Unverified — the specific Claude Code transcript filesystem path and JSONL record schema have not been observed/confirmed in the repo or plan
findings.finding: The plan asserts the adapter reads Claude Code session transcript JSONL and extracts a `ts` field (ISO-8601) plus error-bearing text fields, but never specifies (a) the filesystem path where Claude Code stores its session transcripts or the discovery mechanism, and (b) the JSONL record schema (which field holds the error text, which field holds the timestamp). The repo documents the Codex transcript path (`~/.codex/sessions/YYYY/MM/DD/rollout-*-<id>.jsonl`) but contains no analogous specification for Claude Code transcripts. The implementation cannot locate the files or parse records without this information — these are the core integration surface, not incidentals.
findings.location: Approach section — 'error-signature grep over the transcript JSONL'; Emitted record schema — `"ts": <ISO-8601 of the transcript entry>`; AC1 — 'fixture JSONL transcript'
scenarios: An implementer writing `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py` has no stated path glob to pass as the transcript directory argument — they must independently discover the Claude Code storage layout (e.g., `~/.claude/projects/*/` or another location) before writing any code.

The adapter's `ts` extraction is hard-coded to a JSONL field name the implementer must guess; if the actual Claude Code JSONL uses a different key (e.g., `timestamp`, `created_at`, `time`) the emitted records will be malformed or raise KeyError.

The grep pass targets 'text' content within transcript lines, but Claude Code JSONL records may nest error text inside structured sub-fields (e.g., `{"role": "tool", "content": [{"type": "text", "text": "ModuleNotFoundError..."}]}`), making a naive line-level substring match miss or misclassify entries.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Transcript format' subsection to Approach: name the path glob (e.g., `~/.claude/projects/<hash>/*.jsonl`), specify the relevant JSONL fields (e.g., `content[].text` for message text, `timestamp` for ISO-8601 ts), and note whether the grep pass operates on raw lines or parsed field values. Reference the Claude Code documentation or a sample record. If the schema is not yet verified, mark this as requiring a one-session probe (cat one transcript file) before implementation — this is a low-cost verification, not a full SPIKE.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add ticket 9a5a as a formal `depends_on` of this ticket (so `src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py` exists when AC2 runs) OR move the `_commands/metrics.py` import-absence assertion into ticket 9a5a's own test suite where the file is guaranteed to exist, and remove it from this ticket's AC.
evidence: AC2 text: 'the future `_commands/metrics.py`, once ticket 9a5a lands it, is held to the same rule'
src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py confirmed absent from repo (no match in _commands/ directory listing)
Ticket 9a5a is not listed in this plan's deps/depends_on edges — only referenced informally in the AC text
A source-scan of a non-existent file trivially passes any import-absence assertion
findings.finding: AC2 requires the portability-guard test to assert that `src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py` does not import `adapters.claude_transcripts`, but that file is created by ticket 9a5a which is NOT listed as a `depends_on` of this ticket. Until 9a5a lands, the file does not exist, so the source-scan assertion is vacuously true (or errors on a missing path). The guard is effectively a no-op for THIS ticket's test suite and provides no real enforcement until the dependent artifact lands.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 portability guard; Files section (src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py not in this ticket's file list)
scenarios: This ticket's test suite runs before 9a5a lands: the AC2 guard for _commands/metrics.py passes vacuously, providing zero enforcement.
9a5a lands and inadvertently imports the adapter: this ticket's already-merged test will never re-run to catch the violation, since the guard lives in the backfill ticket's test file, not 9a5a's.
findings.suggested_fix: Move the `_commands/metrics.py` portion of AC2 into ticket 9a5a's AC (which already has a portability-guard AC: 'a test asserts src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py does not import rebar.metrics.adapters'). This ticket's AC2 scope should cover only the files that exist at the time this ticket's tests run: `registry.py` and `metrics/__init__.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an idempotency contract to `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py`: specify a deduplication mechanism (e.g., a seen-set keyed on transcript entry `ts`+`signature`, or a watermark file) so that re-running the script over already-processed transcripts does not emit duplicate classified records.
evidence: The plan describes 'a targeted pass: an error-signature grep over the transcript JSONL ... selects candidate lines, then a deterministic keyword/regex classifier ... maps each signature to the classification enum' with no mention of tracking which transcript entries have already been processed.

The analogous scripts/backfill_bug_class.py in the codebase explicitly implements idempotency via _existing_ticket_ids() deduplication and append-only writes (lines 115-155), demonstrating that this is an established pattern the project uses — and a deliberate omission in the plan.
AC3 requires the test to assert the script 'emits the labeled classified records' but does not assert idempotency on re-run.
ABSENCE: no mention of a 'skip if already processed' check, a seen-set, a watermark, or any resumability mechanism anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no idempotency or resumability mechanism for scripts/backfill_transcripts.py. If the script is interrupted mid-run or re-run after new transcripts accumulate, it will re-process already-classified transcript entries and emit duplicate classified records into the output, with no deduplication guard specified.
findings.location: scripts/backfill_transcripts.py (Files section) and Approach section
scenarios: Operator runs the script, it processes 80% of transcripts and fails (e.g., disk full). On re-run, all 80% are re-processed and duplicated in the output.
Operator runs the script a second time after new transcripts accumulate; previously classified entries are re-emitted, inflating counts in downstream consumers.
findings.suggested_fix: Follow the pattern in scripts/backfill_bug_class.py: write output to a fixed NDJSON artifact (e.g., .rebar/backfill-transcripts.ndjson), read already-emitted (ts, signature) pairs on startup, and skip any candidate that matches an already-seen pair. Assert in AC3 that a second run over the same fixture dir produces 0 new records.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the output path for `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py` (e.g., `.rebar/backfill-transcripts.ndjson`) and define the post-partial-failure state: either the script is atomic (write to temp then rename) or it is append-idempotent with a deduplication guard, so operators know exactly what to do after a failed run.
evidence: The Approach section says 'write labeled snapshots' without naming a file path, directory, or format (stdout vs. NDJSON file vs. per-entry files).
AC1 uses 'jq -e' assertions implying structured JSON output but does not name the file being queried.
AC3 says 'asserts it emits the labeled classified records' without specifying where those records are written.
ABSENCE: no mention of what state the output is in after partial failure, whether a partial file should be deleted, or how an operator recovers — there is no rollback specification.
findings.finding: The plan never specifies the output destination or rollback path for the labeled snapshot records emitted by scripts/backfill_transcripts.py. A partially-completed run leaves an incomplete output artifact with no stated back-out path, and the post-failure schema state is not described.
findings.location: Approach section ('write labeled snapshots'), AC1, AC3
scenarios: Script writes 40 records to an unnamed output file, then crashes. Operator does not know whether to delete the partial file, append to it, or start fresh. The post-failure 'schema state' (output artifact state) is undefined.
findings.suggested_fix: Declare a fixed output path (e.g., `.rebar/backfill-transcripts.ndjson`), write atomically via a temp file + rename, and document in the plan that 'deleting the output file resets the backfill to a clean state.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the output path/naming scheme for emitted snapshots, define idempotency behavior (overwrite vs. skip-existing vs. dedup by `ts`+`signature`), and add a RETIRE/cleanup path (e.g., a `--force` flag, output-dir TTL, or documented one-shot-only contract) so the T9 lifecycle is complete.
evidence: Plan says 'write labeled snapshots' but never names the output directory, file, or naming scheme.
AC3 says the script 'emits the labeled classified records' but specifies no deduplication or overwrite behavior.
No RETIRE or cleanup step appears anywhere in the plan — snapshots accumulate indefinitely if the script is rerun.
T9 checklist: 'There is a RETIRE/cleanup path — it doesn't leak/accumulate' and 'operation idempotent under retry/at-least-once' are both unmet.
findings.finding: The plan never specifies WHERE the emitted labeled snapshots are written (no output path, directory, or file naming convention is given), does not declare whether the backfill script is idempotent (a second invocation could append duplicate records to the same output), and provides no RETIRE/cleanup path for accumulated snapshot files. This leaves the UPDATE concurrency and lifecycle-completeness sub-criteria of T9 unaddressed.
findings.location: ## Approach — 'write labeled snapshots' / ## Acceptance Criteria — AC3 'emits the labeled classified records'
scenarios: A developer re-runs `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py` after adding new transcripts; because no idempotency contract exists, previously classified records are duplicated in the output store, inflating metrics counts.
A CI job or cron that calls the backfill script on each run accumulates unbounded snapshot files with no cleanup, causing storage growth over time.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section to the plan stating: (a) snapshots are written to a single deterministic file (e.g., `.rebar/backfill/transcripts.jsonl`) that is fully overwritten on each run (making the script idempotent by definition); (b) the file is listed in `.gitignore` and can be deleted at any time without affecting the core harness; (c) a `--output` flag allows overriding the path for tests. This closes the output-location, idempotency, and retire sub-criteria simultaneously.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the JSONL field name(s) to read for the `ts` value in the emitted record (e.g., `timestamp`, `created_at`) and specify the fallback (e.g., `null` or omit the field) when the entry has no recognized timestamp key.
evidence: Plan text: '"ts": <ISO-8601 of the transcript entry>' — the extraction rule for this field from the input JSONL is never given.
The plan describes the input as 'transcript JSONL' but never shows an example entry structure or names the timestamp key.
AC1 asserts `jq -e` on emitted records but the field population rule is unspecified.
findings.finding: The output schema field `"ts": <ISO-8601 of the transcript entry>` references a timestamp from the input JSONL, but the plan never specifies which JSONL field name to read (e.g., `timestamp`, `created_at`, `ts`, or some other key), nor a fallback when the entry lacks a timestamp. An executor implementing the adapter must invent the field name, and AC1's `jq -e` assertion on `.ts` will pass or fail depending on that invention.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Emitted record JSON schema'; AC1
scenarios: Executor reads a field named `timestamp` from the JSONL entry; actual Claude Code transcripts use `created_at` or `time` — the field is null/missing and the test fails.
Two executors independently implement different field names, producing incompatible output schemas.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'The adapter reads the transcript entry's timestamp from the field named `timestamp` (falling back to `created_at` if absent, else `null`); the emitted `ts` is that value coerced to ISO-8601 string or `null` if unavailable.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `none` enum contradiction: either (a) clarify that `none` is an internal enum value used during classification but never written to output records (and the `kind` field in the JSON schema only accepts the four non-none values), or (b) define `none` as an emitted `kind` value for explicitly-flagged non-events and update the clean-transcript assertion accordingly.
evidence: Plan text: '"kind": <one of the enum values>' in the output schema — `none` is one of the enum values.
Plan text: '`none` ← a candidate line matching no rule above (dropped, not emitted)' — `none` records should not be emitted.
AC4: 'test asserts the enum has exactly these values' including `none` — making `none` part of the enum's public contract.
AC1: 'a clean transcript yields 0' — consistent with non-emission of `none`, but the contradiction is not resolved.
findings.finding: The `none` enum value is simultaneously declared as part of the closed classification enum (AC4 asserts the enum has exactly `env_setup, dependency, integration, tooling, none`) and defined as a non-emitted internal sentinel in the routing table ('dropped, not emitted'). This creates a structural contradiction: the output schema says `"kind": <one of the enum values>` but `none` must never appear in emitted records. An executor cannot determine whether `none` is a valid `kind` in output records or purely an internal routing label.
findings.location: Approach section — routing table ('none ← ... dropped, not emitted'); Approach section — 'Emitted record JSON schema'; AC4
scenarios: Executor reads the schema as permitting `kind: none` in output and emits such records — the 'clean transcript yields 0' assertion fails.
Executor omits `none` from the Python enum class to make the schema consistent with non-emission — AC4's 'exactly these values' assertion fails.
Future consumer of the output schema allows `kind: none` in its parser, silently accepting dropped-event noise.
findings.suggested_fix: Preferred: add a note in the Approach section — 'The Python enum includes `none` as an internal routing sentinel; emitted records carry only `env_setup | dependency | integration | tooling` in the `kind` field. The output schema's `kind` type is constrained to these four values.' Update the schema line to `"kind": <env_setup | dependency | integration | tooling>`.

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default transcript-dir path (e.g. `~/.claude/projects/`) and/or specify the CLI flag name (`--transcript-dir PATH`) in the plan so the executor does not have to invent the script's interface.
evidence: Plan says 'mines this machine's Claude Code session transcripts' but never specifies the default path (e.g. `~/.claude/projects/` or a CLI flag `--transcript-dir`)
No CLI argument schema or env-var name is defined for the script
findings.finding: The default filesystem path where Claude Code session transcripts are stored on the local machine is not stated anywhere in the plan. An executor writing `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py` must either hard-code a path, accept a CLI argument, or read an env var — but none of these is specified, so the executor must make an undirected design choice.
findings.location: scripts/backfill_transcripts.py description; 'Approach' section
scenarios: Executor hard-codes a path that differs from the real location, breaking the script silently
Executor adds a required positional arg but AC3 fixture test passes a different path — test passes but production usage is undocumented
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py` description: 'Accepts `--transcript-dir PATH` (default: `~/.claude/projects/`); walks all `*.jsonl` files found recursively under that directory.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define an idempotency strategy for `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py` writes (e.g., output to a deduplicated JSONL keyed on `ts`+`signature`, skip-if-exists check, or a manifest of processed transcript files) and add an AC assertion that re-running over the same fixture dir produces no duplicate records.
evidence: 'write labeled snapshots' is mentioned but no deduplication, output-file existence check, or upsert strategy is specified
AC line 3 only checks that records are emitted, not that re-runs are safe
Absence of any idempotency language in the plan
findings.finding: The backfill script writes labeled snapshot records but the plan specifies no idempotency mechanism — running the script twice on the same transcript directory would produce duplicate records, corrupting the snapshot store.
findings.location: scripts/backfill_transcripts.py — Approach section ('write labeled snapshots') and AC line 3
scenarios: Developer re-runs backfill after adding more transcripts
Backfill script is accidentally run twice in CI or a dev workflow
findings.suggested_fix: Track processed transcript file paths in a manifest file (e.g., `.backfill_manifest.json`); skip files already in the manifest. Alternatively, key output records on `(ts, signature)` and deduplicate before writing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error handling for the backfill script's file I/O path: malformed/truncated JSONL lines must be skipped with a logged warning (not a hard crash), and a missing transcript directory should produce a clear error message and exit gracefully.
evidence: 'an error-signature grep over the transcript JSONL' — no mention of handling malformed or truncated JSONL lines
No mention of what happens when the transcript directory is absent or empty
No mention of logging warnings/errors for skipped/malformed entries
Absence of any try/except or graceful-degradation language
findings.finding: The plan specifies no error handling for file I/O failures in the transcript adapter or backfill script (malformed JSONL lines, missing transcript directory, unreadable files) — these are real failure points with no stated degradation strategy.
findings.location: Approach section — 'error-signature grep over the transcript JSONL'; scripts/backfill_transcripts.py
scenarios: A transcript file is partially written and contains a truncated JSON line
The configured transcript directory does not exist on the machine
A transcript file has unexpected encoding
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap each JSONL line parse in a try/except, log a warning with line number and file path, and continue. Guard the transcript directory existence at script startup with a clear user-facing error.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Standardize on one term ('interval' or 'snapshot') for the emitted output entity throughout the plan (Approach, Files, Tests, and all AC lines), and confirm the chosen term matches what the `jq -e` assertions in AC1 and AC3 will actually target.
evidence: Plan text (Tests): 'extracted and classified into an `env_setup` interval'
Plan text (AC1): 'classified into at least one interval tagged `source=backfill_classified`, `confidence=classified`'
Plan text (Approach): 'write labeled snapshots'
Plan text (Files): 'targeted extraction ... -> classify -> write labeled snapshots'
The emitted record JSON schema is given no explicit entity name — neither 'interval' nor 'snapshot' appears in the schema definition itself.
findings.finding: The plan uses 'interval' (Tests section, AC1) and 'snapshot' (Files description, Approach section: 'write labeled snapshots') as names for the emitted output entity, but never equates the two terms or defines whether they are the same artifact or distinct outputs at different pipeline stages. An executor cannot determine the canonical name of the output record type.
findings.location: Tests section ('classified into at least one interval'); AC1 ('interval tagged source=backfill_classified'); Approach section ('write labeled snapshots'); Files section ('write labeled snapshots')
scenarios: Executor implements two separate output types (an intermediate 'interval' and a final persisted 'snapshot'), adding unnecessary complexity.
Executor uses one term internally and the other in test fixture keys, causing AC1's `jq -e` assertions to mismatch field paths.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test fixture containing at least one malformed/non-JSON line interleaved with valid entries and assert the script skips the bad line and still emits records for the valid matching lines (or documents the intended behavior if crashing is acceptable).
evidence: AC3 requires the script be invoked over a fixture transcript dir but only asserts it 'emits the labeled classified records' — no mention of a malformed-input fixture
The Approach section defines the JSONL source and grep pass but says nothing about error handling for invalid lines
Claude Code session transcripts are real-world files that may contain partial writes or unexpected formats
findings.finding: The test plan does not address malformed or truncated JSONL lines in the transcript (e.g. a partially-written entry, a line that is valid JSON but missing the expected timestamp field, or a non-JSON line). The adapter/script behavior (crash vs. skip-and-continue) is unspecified, and no AC exercises this failure path for the user-invoked backfill script.
findings.location: ## Tests / ## Acceptance Criteria (AC3) — backfill_transcripts.py entry-point test
scenarios: A session transcript ends mid-write (power loss); the script crashes with a JSON decode error and emits nothing rather than processing the valid preceding lines
A transcript line is valid JSON but has no `ts` field; the script raises a KeyError, aborting the entire backfill run

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconsider placing `claude_transcripts.py` directly in `scripts/` (co-located with `backfill_transcripts.py`, matching the `backfill_bug_class.py` precedent) rather than creating a new `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/` sub-package; if the `adapters/` package is retained, add a concrete committed second use case (not hypothetical) to justify the structural layer.
evidence: Plan states: 'imported only by the backfill script, never by the core command'
Plan states: 'a parallel adapter could target another harness' — a hypothetical, not a committed second use case
Codebase precedent: `scripts/backfill_bug_class.py` is a self-contained classifier script in `scripts/` with no corresponding `src/` sub-package
The `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/` directory does not exist in the codebase and would be created solely for this one module
findings.finding: The plan creates a new `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/` package sub-directory for a single module with exactly one consumer (`scripts/backfill_transcripts.py`), justified only by a hypothetical future second adapter. The codebase's existing precedent for one-time backfill scripts (`scripts/backfill_bug_class.py`) is a self-contained script with classifier logic co-located in `scripts/`, not an `src/` sub-package. Placing `claude_transcripts.py` directly in `scripts/` as a helper module (or inlining it) is strictly simpler, better-aligned with the existing codebase pattern, and reduces blast-radius (no new permanent package namespace in the metrics tree). The hypothetical 'parallel adapter could target another harness' is not a concrete, committed use case and does not justify the structural overhead.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/__init__.py`, `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/claude_transcripts.py`; Approach section: 'a parallel adapter could target another harness'
scenarios: An implementer following the plan creates a permanent `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/` package that future code must navigate around, even if the hypothetical second adapter never materialises.
The portability guard in AC2 must guard against imports from this new namespace in perpetuity, even though it is a one-time backfill artifact.
findings.suggested_fix: Move classifier logic into `scripts/` (e.g. as `scripts/_transcript_classifier.py` or inlined in `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py`), matching the `scripts/backfill_bug_class.py` pattern. This eliminates the new package namespace while preserving full isolation from the core metrics package.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge scripts/backfill_bug_class.py as prior art implementing the identical backfill-classified pattern, and either align backfill_transcripts.py with that convention (plain script under scripts/, no adapters/ sub-package) or add an explicit justification for why the new adapter sub-package abstraction is warranted over the established approach.
evidence: Plan names 'src/rebar/metrics/adapters/__init__.py', 'src/rebar/metrics/adapters/claude_transcripts.py' as new files
scripts/backfill_bug_class.py already exists: same pattern (deterministic classifier, source='backfill_classified', confidence='classified', standalone script, no adapter package)
tests/scripts/test_backfill_bug_class.py and test_backfill_bug_class_heldout.py establish the test convention for this pattern
Plan contains no mention of backfill_bug_class.py or rationale for diverging from the established scripts/ convention
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new src/rebar/metrics/adapters/ sub-package abstraction for what the codebase already handles as a plain standalone script under scripts/. scripts/backfill_bug_class.py implements the identical pattern (deterministic keyword classifier, source=backfill_classified, confidence=classified, standalone script, no adapter layer) and is the established prior art; the plan neither mentions it nor justifies the architectural divergence (new package namespace vs. plain script).
findings.location: Files section / Approach section — src/rebar/metrics/adapters/__init__.py and src/rebar/metrics/adapters/claude_transcripts.py
scenarios: An implementer builds a new adapters/ package for a single one-time-use file when the established pattern is a plain scripts/ file; this creates an inconsistency the next contributor must reconcile.
A future backfill script author follows the wrong pattern (adapter package vs. standalone script) because both exist with no guidance on which is canonical.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the src/rebar/metrics/adapters/ sub-package. Place the classifier logic directly in scripts/backfill_transcripts.py following the structure of scripts/backfill_bug_class.py: a classify_backfill() callable + main() entry point, with tests under tests/scripts/. This makes the new script consistent with the established convention and eliminates the novel architecture concern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the `adapters/` package sub-directory against YAGNI: if no second adapter is planned within this ticket, consider placing `claude_transcripts.py` directly under `src/rebar/metrics/` (or a `backfill/` folder) to avoid a one-member package abstraction with a single call-site.
evidence: Plan states: 'a parallel adapter could target another harness' as rationale for the adapters/ directory
Only 1 call-site exists in current criteria: `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py`
G5 checklist: 'Proposed structure is justified by current criteria (>=3 real call-sites for any new abstraction)'
findings.finding: The `adapters/` package sub-directory introduces a structural layer (package + __init__) justified only by a hypothetical future adapter ('a parallel adapter could target another harness'), not by current criteria — there is exactly 1 real call-site (the backfill script). Per YAGNI/Rule-of-Three, a new package abstraction needs ≥3 real call-sites to be justified by current criteria.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/__init__.py`, `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/claude_transcripts.py`
scenarios: The adapters/ package layer adds indirection with no current payoff — future adapters may never materialize, leaving orphaned structure.
findings.suggested_fix: Move `claude_transcripts.py` to `src/rebar/metrics/backfill_transcripts_adapter.py` (or similar flat location) and drop the `adapters/` package until a second adapter warrants the directory. Update the portability-guard test path accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/` sub-package layer is justified by ≥2 current (not hypothetical) adapters; if not, flatten to a single module (e.g., `src/rebar/metrics/claude_transcripts.py`) — the portability/isolation goal is satisfied without the speculative namespace.
evidence: Grep confirms `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/` does not exist anywhere in the codebase — zero existing call-sites or consumers.
The plan's only justification for the sub-package layer is: 'a parallel adapter could target another harness' — a hypothetical future need, not a current done-definition.
Rule-of-Three: 0 existing adapters in this namespace, 1 proposed. The threshold of ≥3 call-sites or concrete current need is not met.
The portability guard AC (AC2) only requires that the core package does NOT import the adapter — satisfied equally by a flat module without a sub-package.
findings.finding: The `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/` sub-package (with its own `__init__.py`) is a premature abstraction: it introduces a new directory/namespace layer for exactly one file, justified only by a hypothetical future adapter ('a parallel adapter could target another harness'). No existing call-sites for this namespace exist in the codebase (`src/rebar/metrics/adapters/` is absent from the repo), and the isolation requirement (core does not import the adapter) is equally satisfied by a flat module at `src/rebar/metrics/claude_transcripts.py`. This is a Rule-of-Three violation (0 existing callers, 1 proposed file) and a YAGNI violation (the multi-adapter namespace serves a hypothetical, not a current done-definition).
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/__init__.py`, `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/claude_transcripts.py`; Approach section: 'a parallel adapter could target another harness'
scenarios: Executor creates `adapters/__init__.py` + `adapters/claude_transcripts.py` adding a namespace that may never gain a second occupant, leaving permanent speculative scaffolding.
A future reviewer sees `metrics/adapters/` and assumes it is a stable extension point, adding more adapters there before the pattern is validated.
findings.suggested_fix: Drop `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/__init__.py`; rename the adapter to `src/rebar/metrics/claude_transcripts.py` (imported only by the backfill script). The AC2 portability guard text would need to reference `rebar.metrics.claude_transcripts` instead of `adapters.claude_transcripts`.

findings.finding: The decision rules mapping error-signature patterns to enum values are never specified. The plan names 7+ grep patterns (LLMUnavailableError, ConnectionError, ModuleNotFoundError, 'command not found', 'No such file', HTTP 429, 'timed out') and 5 enum values but provides zero routing rules for which pattern maps to which class. An implementer must invent the mapping, making the classifier non-deterministic across implementers and the unit test unverifiable without a normative table.
findings.location: Approach section — 'a deterministic keyword/regex classifier … maps each signature to the classification enum'
findings.suggested_fix:
Add a table to the Approach section, e.g.:
| Pattern | Enum value |
|---|---|
| ModuleNotFoundError | dependency |
| command not found | tooling |
| No such file | env_setup |
| connection refused / ConnectionError | integration |
| HTTP 429 / timed out | integration |
| LLMUnavailableError | integration |
And specify tie-breaking rules for lines matching multiple patterns.

findings.finding: The JSON output schema for emitted records is referenced but never field-specified. The plan says 'the JSON output schema are defined in the adapter module' and AC4 asserts the enum is defined there, but no field names (beyond source and confidence) are specified anywhere in the plan — not timestamp, not pattern_matched, not raw_excerpt, not classification. AC1's jq -e assertion cannot be written by an implementer without inventing the schema.
findings.location: Approach section — 'The classification enum … and the JSON output schema are defined in the adapter module'; AC1 — 'jq -e'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a record schema block to the Approach section, e.g.: {"source": "backfill_classified", "confidence": "classified", "classification": "<enum value>", "pattern": "<matched pattern string>", "excerpt": "<matched line text>", "transcript_file": "<path>", "line_number": <int>}

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit cardinality proving command for the 'clean transcript yields 0' branch of AC1, e.g. `assert $(cat output.jsonl | wc -l) -eq 0` or `python -c 'import json,sys; recs=json.load(sys.stdin); assert len(recs)==0'`.
evidence: AC1 specifies `jq -e` assertions targeting `.kind` and `.source`/`.confidence` for the positive case
The zero-records assertion 'yields 0' has no corresponding `jq` or `assert` command — `jq -e` on empty output is an error, not a '0 records' assertion
Taxonomy item #5: missing cardinality assertion when the criterion names a count
findings.finding: AC1 states 'a clean transcript yields 0' but does not specify a cardinality-asserting proving command. The `jq -e` pattern targets `.kind`/`.source`/`.confidence` fields on individual records — it would error on empty output rather than affirmatively assert zero records. This is taxonomy defect #5 (missing cardinality assertion when the criterion names a count).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1: 'a clean transcript yields 0'
scenarios: A broken classifier emits no records for ANY transcript (including the failing one), and the clean-transcript branch of the test still appears to 'pass' because both branches produce empty output with no distinguishing assertion
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC1 to read: '…a clean transcript yields 0 classified records (assert `jq ". | length == 0" output.jsonl` or equivalent `len(records) == 0` in the unit test fixture assert).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make the pattern→enum routing table externally configurable (e.g., loaded from a TOML/YAML config file or a per-project override), OR explicitly document in the module that the table is intentionally frozen and extension requires a code change — and capture this design decision in AGENTS.md or an ADR.
evidence: The full routing table is specified as a static, hardcoded list in the plan
No mention of a config file, environment variable, or plugin point for extending/overriding patterns
'first match wins; case-insensitive substring/regex' — purely hard-coded dispatch
findings.finding: The normative pattern→enum routing table is hardcoded in the adapter module with no configurability mechanism, yet error signatures are inherently subject to change as the codebase and toolchain evolve.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Normative pattern→enum routing table'
scenarios: A new dependency manager replaces pip/uv, introducing new error signatures
A new integration target introduces new HTTP error patterns not in the table
A user on a different OS sees different error messages that don't match the hardcoded strings

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AGENTS.md entry (or a brief ADR) documenting the 'backfill adapter quarantine' architectural decision: why the adapter must never be imported by core modules, and what the import-guard test protects.
evidence: No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan
The isolation-via-backfill-script pattern is non-obvious and warrants documentation so future contributors understand why the adapter must never be imported by core modules
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern (transcript-mining backfill adapter with a quarantine boundary enforced via import-guard tests) but specifies no ADR or AGENTS.md update to capture this decision for future maintainers.
findings.location: Approach section — 'the adapter is optional and quarantined behind the backfill script'
scenarios: A future contributor adds the adapter as a convenience import in the core metrics module, breaking the portability guarantee
A new team member cannot understand why the quarantine test exists without documentation
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section to AGENTS.md: 'Transcript adapter isolation: `adapters/claude_transcripts.py` is intentionally quarantined — imported only by `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py`. The import-guard test in `test_transcript_adapter.py` enforces this boundary. Do not import from core metrics modules.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add AC / test cases for each of the remaining three active enum branches: at least one fixture line that should route to `dependency` (e.g. `Could not find a version`), one to `integration` (e.g. `connection refused`), and one to `tooling` (e.g. `ruff` invocation failure) — assert each emits the correct `.kind`.
evidence: AC1 specifies only ModuleNotFoundError → env_setup as the fixture scenario
The Approach section defines a full 4-branch routing table: env_setup, dependency, integration, tooling
No AC or test description mentions a ConnectionError, pip-resolution-error, or git-error fixture
findings.finding: The normative pattern→enum routing table defines four active classification branches (env_setup, dependency, integration, tooling), but the test plan and ACs only exercise the `env_setup` branch (via a ModuleNotFoundError fixture). The `dependency`, `integration`, and `tooling` routing rules have no corresponding test assertions, so bugs in those three branches — the majority of the classifier's logic — would go undetected while all ACs pass green.
findings.location: ## Tests / ## Acceptance Criteria (AC1) — 'a fixture JSONL transcript containing a known env-failure signature (e.g. a ModuleNotFoundError line)'
scenarios: A typo in the `integration` branch regex (e.g. misspelled 'refused') silently misclassifies all ConnectionError lines as `none`; the suite stays green
The `dependency` branch pattern list omits `uv` resolution errors; no test catches the gap
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the unit test with three additional fixture JSONL entries (one per missing branch) and assert their classified `.kind` values. A single parametrized test over [("ModuleNotFoundError", "env_setup"), ("connection refused", "integration"), ("Could not find a version", "dependency"), ("Permission denied", "tooling")] covers all active branches with minimal duplication.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
